description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).
teaching_value: Gives students a mental map of the entire agent system before diving into individual components, making it easier to contextualize each subsystem (planning, memory, tools) as part of a coherent whole.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure; return to it as a reference when introducing each individual component.

description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how interleaving reasoning traces with actions differs from action-only approaches, helping students understand why the Thought step improves performance on both knowledge retrieval and sequential decision tasks.
when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use to contrast Act-only vs. ReAct trajectories and prompt discussion on why explicit reasoning steps matter.
suggested_caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)

description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle of: Actor generating actions, Evaluator scoring trajectories, Self-Reflection module producing verbal feedback, and that feedback being stored in episodic memory to inform the next trial.
teaching_value: Visualizes the feedback loop that enables iterative self-improvement in agents, making the abstract concept of 'dynamic memory and self-reflection' concrete by showing how each component feeds into the next.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of self-reflection; use the diagram to walk through each stage of the Reflexion loop step by step.
suggested_caption: Reflexion framework: actor, evaluator, and self-reflection cycle. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across successive trials.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for Reflexion's effectiveness and distinguishes between two distinct failure modes (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), helping students understand what self-reflection specifically addresses.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to ground the theory in experimental results; use to discuss the relative prevalence of hallucination vs. planning failures.
suggested_caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates AlfWorld failures. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)

description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, Best) is fed as input, and the model learns to produce progressively improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.
teaching_value: Makes the CoH training procedure tangible by showing how ranked feedback sequences are structured as input, clarifying how the model learns to self-improve by conditioning on its own annotated history.
when_to_show: Introduce when teaching CoH as a self-reflection mechanism; use to contrast with RLHF and highlight how explicit feedback sequences differ from scalar reward signals.
suggested_caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns from ranked, annotated output sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)

description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source RL policies generate multi-episode learning histories per task, which are concatenated and used to train a single transformer via behavioral cloning to perform in-context RL.
teaching_value: Clarifies the non-obvious idea that a learning algorithm itself can be distilled into a neural network by showing how multi-episode histories encode the improvement signal that the transformer learns to replicate.
when_to_show: Use when introducing in-context RL and AD; the diagram helps students grasp why multi-episode context is necessary and how the distillation process works.
suggested_caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² on memory-and-exploration environments (dark rooms, watermaze), showing AD matches or exceeds RL² with binary rewards only.
teaching_value: Provides quantitative validation that in-context RL via AD is competitive with dedicated RL algorithms, helping students evaluate the practical viability of the approach across different environment types.
when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to substantiate its claims; prompt students to compare AD vs. RL² and discuss what the results imply about context length requirements.
suggested_caption: AD matches RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward only. (Laskin et al., 2023)

description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into Sensory Memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), Short-Term/Working Memory, and Long-Term Memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.
teaching_value: Bridges cognitive science and AI by mapping biological memory types to their LLM agent counterparts (e.g., in-context window as working memory, vector stores as long-term memory), giving students a framework to reason about agent memory design.
when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the memory module; use the biological analogy to motivate design choices for agent memory systems before discussing technical implementations.
suggested_caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)

description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.
teaching_value: Illustrates the practical tradeoff between retrieval accuracy and speed in vector databases, helping students understand why approximate methods are chosen over exact search for large-scale agent memory retrieval.
when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory retrieval mechanisms; use to motivate the choice of ANN over exact search and to compare specific algorithm tradeoffs.
suggested_caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoff. (Google Blog, 2020)

description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how an LLM can orchestrate multiple specialized models as tools, making abstract agent architecture tangible by showing task decomposition, dependency tracking, and multi-model coordination in a single visual flow.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, especially to demonstrate how a single LLM can coordinate many external models rather than solving everything itself.
suggested_caption: HuggingGPT: LLM orchestrates specialized models across four pipeline stages. (Shen et al. 2023)

description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark: checking API availability, forming the call, executing it, and evaluating the result at multiple decision points.
teaching_value: Shows students the concrete decision-making steps an LLM agent must perform to use APIs correctly, making the abstract concept of 'tool use capability' measurable and debuggable through explicit evaluation checkpoints.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how to evaluate or benchmark an agent's tool use ability, or when explaining the granular decisions an LLM must make correctly to successfully call an external API.
suggested_caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for tool API calls. (Li et al. 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of a generative agent showing three interconnected components: a memory stream (log of experiences), a reflection mechanism (synthesizing higher-level insights), and a planning module, all feeding into the agent's actions within a simulated environment.
teaching_value: Illustrates how memory, reflection, and planning work together as distinct but integrated modules in a believable autonomous agent, helping students understand why each component is necessary for coherent long-term behavior.
when_to_show: Show when teaching memory and planning components of LLM agents, or when discussing how agents maintain coherent identity and behavior over extended interactions in a simulation.
suggested_caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning modules. (Park et al. 2023)

description: A flow diagram showing an AI agent loop: a user asks a question (e.g., weather in Paris), the LLM decides it needs external data, generates a structured function/tool call with parameters (city=Paris), sends it to an external API/tool, receives the tool result, and then the LLM composes a final natural-language answer back to the user.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the end-to-end mechanics of function calling: how the model transitions from interpreting intent to selecting a tool, forming correct arguments, using returned data, and producing a grounded response—clarifying why tools turn an LLM into an agent.
when_to_show: Show when introducing why LLMs need tools for real-time/authoritative data, or when walking through a concrete tool-calling example (weather lookup) to map each step from user query to tool call to final response.
suggested_caption: Function-calling agent flow: interpret, call tool, use results, respond (DAIR.AI).
